ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for Tom Yum Prawn Rice exists; closest_parent 'rice dish' (FOODON_00002572) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Rice dishes) is the closest parent category; no specific code for Tom Yum Prawn Rice exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific Thai rice dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200 g of cooked jasmine rice and 200 g of Tom Yum prawn broth with 4–6 medium prawns.

nutrient_source.title: Tom Yum Kung (Spicy Prawn Soup) and Steamed Jasmine Rice — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Shrimp, cooked (NDB 15152), Rice, white, cooked (NDB 20444), and Tom Yum soup base components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Tom Yum Prawn Rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked white rice (~50% by weight), cooked shrimp (~20% by weight), and Tom Yum broth components (lemongrass, galangal, chilli, fish sauce, lime juice, mushrooms, ~30% by weight). Proportions are based on standard Thai restaurant plating. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce in the broth. Values represent a per-100 g weighted composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice (boiled) GI ~64 (glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yum Prawn Rice as a composite dish. GI of 64 is assigned based on the dominant glycemic driver being boiled white jasmine rice, for which GI ~64 (medium) is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The prawn and broth components contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 74.0) / 100 = 47.4, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving ≈ 74 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and Tom Yum broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (≈1680 mg per standard 400 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white jasmine rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish; the white jasmine rice base contributes a high glycemic load of approximately 47 per serving (GI 64, ~74 g carbohydrate per serving), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and substituting brown rice or reducing portion size is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the high sodium content (~1680 mg per serving) and moderate cholesterol contribution from prawns (~248 mg per serving), both of which may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; the low saturated fat content (0.3 g per 100 g) is a mitigating factor.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yum Prawn Rice (Thai: ข้าวต้มยำกุ้ง, romanized: Khao Tom Yum Kung) is a Thai rice dish consisting of steamed or boiled jasmine rice served with or immersed in a bold, tangy, and spicy Tom Yum broth loaded with juicy prawns. The Tom Yum broth is a hallmark of Thai cuisine, prepared by simmering lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, fresh chillies, mushrooms, fish sauce, and lime juice to create a complex sour-spicy-savoury flavour profile. The prawns are typically poached directly in the broth, absorbing the aromatic spices. The dish is a satisfying one-bowl meal combining the mild, starchy base of jasmine rice with the intensely flavoured seafood broth. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein from prawns (~6.8 g per 100 g), predominantly carbohydrate energy from rice (~18.5 g per 100 g), and low fat (~1.4 g per 100 g). However, sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to fish sauce, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is medium (~64) driven by white rice, resulting in a high glycemic load per standard serving (~47), which is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically halal. It is commonly served in Thai restaurants across Singapore, including at Saap Saap Thai outlets.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Tom Yum Prawn Rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked white rice, cooked shrimp, and Tom Yum broth ingredients using standard Thai restaurant proportions. GI is assigned by analogy to boiled white jasmine rice from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the proportions are well-grounded in standard Thai culinary practice.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked white rice (NDB 20444), cooked shrimp (NDB 15152), and Tom Yum broth components; no single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. 2. Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce in the Tom Yum broth; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 3. GI of 64 is assigned by analogy to boiled white jasmine rice per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); the prawn and broth components contribute negligible glycemic load. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this composite estimate. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this composite estimate. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the primary components of this dish. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this composite estimate. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this composite estimate. 9. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in the component foods per USDA SR Legacy data. 10. The food_name has been normalised from the menu label 'T5 Prawns Rice' to the canonical English name 'Tom Yum Prawn Rice'; the original menu label is preserved in aliases. 11. Thai mandatory local-language name (ข้าวต้มยำกุ้ง) is present per G6 multilingual compliance rules for Thai cuisine.
